subject: Chemistry
unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.question: How do the boiling points of structural isomers of pentane (\(\mathrm{C_5H_{12}}\)) compare, and why?

options.A: They are identical because they have the same molecular formula
options.B: The branched isomer has the highest boiling point due to stronger London forces
options.C: The straight-chain isomer (n-pentane) has the highest boiling point due to greater surface area
options.D: All isomers have the same boiling point because they are nonpolar

questions.hint: Consider how molecular shape affects intermolecular contact.
questions.explanation: n-Pentane has a larger surface area for London dispersion forces than branched isomers, resulting in stronger intermolecular attraction. Option B is incorrect because branching decreases surface area and boiling point.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.content_anchor: constitutional isomers have different physical properties

explanations_tiered.definition: Structural isomers are compounds with the same molecular formula but different atom connectivity.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Isomers have different physical properties. Straight-chain alkanes have higher boiling points than branched isomers because they can pack more closely together.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of straight spaghetti (sticks together) vs. curly spaghetti (falls apart easily).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: n-Pentane has a linear shape, maximizing van der Waals contacts and London forces, requiring more energy to separate. Branched isomers have a compact shape, reducing intermolecular contact.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The boiling point trend is governed by molecular surface area. Branching reduces the surface area available for London dispersion forces, lowering the boiling point despite identical molar mass.
reasoning_steps: Identify the isomers of pentane (n-pentane, isopentane, neopentane).
Compare their molecular shapes (linear vs branched).
Relate shape to London dispersion force strength.
misconceptions: Assuming that higher branching increases boiling point due to 'more branches'.

glossary_entry.definition: Molecules with identical molecular formulas but different bonding arrangements.

flashcard.front: Which pentane isomer has the highest boiling point?
flashcard.back: n-Pentane (straight chain), due to larger surface area for London forces.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why does n-pentane have a higher boiling point than neopentane?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: n-Pentane has greater surface area, leading to stronger London dispersion forces.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Isomerism arising from different connectivity of atoms.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use molecular models to visualize surface area differences.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain isomerism and draw isomers of alkanes
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Straight chain maximizes surface area and London forces.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.question: How many structural isomers exist for the alkane with the molecular formula \(\mathrm{C_6H_{14}}\)?
questions.hint: Recall the table of alkane isomers for increasing carbon chains.
questions.explanation: Hexane (\(\mathrm{C_6H_{14}}\)) has five structural isomers: n-hexane, 2-methylpentane, 3-methylpentane, 2,2-dimethylbutane, and 2,3-dimethylbutane. Option D (9) is the number of isomers for heptane (\(\mathrm{C_7H_{16}}\)).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES

explanations_tiered.definition: A structural isomer has the same molecular formula but a different arrangement of carbon atoms.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The number of possible structural isomers increases rapidly with carbon count. Hexane (\(\mathrm{C_6H_{14}}\)) has exactly five distinct structural isomers.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Draw a 6-carbon chain. Try moving one carbon to a branch; keep going until you can't make a new shape.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The five isomers are: (1) straight chain, (2) methyl on C2, (3) methyl on C3, (4) two methyls on C2, (5) methyls on C2 and C3.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Using systematic structural generation avoiding duplicates yields exactly five isomers for \(\mathrm{C_6H_{14}}\). The formula \(2^{n-4}+1\) approximates but underestimates for higher n.
reasoning_steps: Start with the straight chain (n-hexane).
Add a methyl branch in all possible unique positions.
Add two methyl branches, ensuring no longer chain exists.
Count unique structures.

glossary_entry.definition: Compounds with the same molecular formula but different structural formulas.

flashcard.front: How many structural isomers does hexane (\(\mathrm{C_6H_{14}}\)) have?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The existence of compounds with the same formula but different structures.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Draw the possible structural isomers for alkanes
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Hexane has five structural isomers.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.question: The Wurtz reaction involves the reaction of an alkyl halide with sodium metal. Which alkane is produced when methyl chloride (\(\mathrm{CH_3Cl}\)) reacts with sodium?
questions.hint: Wurtz synthesis couples two alkyl halides to form a larger alkane.
questions.explanation: The Wurtz reaction couples two methyl groups: \(2\mathrm{CH_3Cl} + 2\mathrm{Na} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH_3CH_3} + 2\mathrm{NaCl}\). Option A would require no coupling, which does not occur.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES

explanations_tiered.definition: The Wurtz reaction is a coupling reaction where two alkyl halides react with sodium to form a higher alkane.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Two methyl chloride molecules couple, losing sodium chloride, to form a carbon-carbon bond, yielding ethane.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Two methyl pieces snap together to make a longer chain (ethane).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The reaction is: \(2\mathrm{CH_3Cl} + 2\mathrm{Na} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH_3CH_3} + 2\mathrm{NaCl}\). The two methyl radicals combine.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The mechanism involves the formation of alkyl radicals (\(\mathrm{CH_3\cdot}\)) which dimerize. It is limited to symmetric coupling and works best for primary halides.
reasoning_steps: Recall the Wurtz reaction couples two alkyl halides.
Identify the alkyl group from methyl chloride (CH3-).
Couple two CH3 groups to get CH3CH3 (ethane).
misconceptions: Assuming methyl chloride gives methane (would require reduction, not coupling).

glossary_entry.definition: The coupling of two alkyl halides using sodium metal to form a higher alkane.

flashcard.front: What product forms from methyl chloride in the Wurtz reaction?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What alkane is made from methyl chloride via Wurtz?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A method to prepare alkanes by reacting alkyl halides with sodium.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Note: Wurtz fails for tertiary halides.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe methods for preparation of alkanes
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Two methyl groups couple to form ethane.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.question: Methane can be prepared in the laboratory by heating sodium acetate with soda lime. What type of reaction is this?
questions.hint: The carboxylate group (\(\mathrm{COO^-}\)) is removed as carbon dioxide.
questions.explanation: Decarboxylation removes a carboxyl group as \(\mathrm{CO_2}\). Sodium acetate loses \(\mathrm{CO_2}\) to form methane. Option A, hydrogenation, adds hydrogen, which does not occur here.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES

explanations_tiered.definition: Decarboxylation is a reaction that removes a carboxyl group (\(\mathrm{CO_2}\)) from a molecule.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Heating sodium acetate (\(\mathrm{CH_3COONa}\)) with soda lime (\(\mathrm{NaOH/CaO}\)) removes \(\mathrm{CO_2}\), leaving methane.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The molecule 'pops off' a carbon dioxide bubble, leaving a smaller hydrocarbon.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The reaction is \(\mathrm{CH_3COONa + NaOH \rightarrow CH_4 + Na_2CO_3}\). The \(\mathrm{COO}\) group is replaced by H from NaOH.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Decarboxylation of carboxylic acid salts is an elimination of \(\mathrm{CO_2}\). The mechanism requires the \(\alpha\)-carbon to be relatively stable (as in acetate), producing an alkane one carbon shorter.
reasoning_steps: Identify the functional group being removed (carboxyl).
Recall the definition of decarboxylation (removal of CO2).
Match to the reaction type.
misconceptions: Confusing decarboxylation with simple combustion or neutralization.

glossary_entry.definition: The removal of a carboxyl group as carbon dioxide from a molecule.

flashcard.front: What reaction produces methane from sodium acetate?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How is methane made from sodium acetate?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A reaction where carbon dioxide is lost from a molecule.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize the role of soda lime as a base and dehydrating agent.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. The removal of CO2 is decarboxylation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.question: Why are alkanes often called 'paraffins' and are generally unreactive toward acids and bases?

options.A: They contain double bonds that stabilize the molecule
options.B: They are polar and form strong hydrogen bonds
options.C: They have only strong \(\mathrm{C-C}\) and \(\mathrm{C-H}\) single bonds and are nonpolar
options.D: They exist only as gases at room temperature

questions.hint: Consider the bond strength and polarity.
questions.explanation: The \(\mathrm{C-C}\) and \(\mathrm{C-H}\) bonds are strong and the molecules are nonpolar, making them resistant to attack. Option A is false; alkanes lack double bonds (they are saturated).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES

explanations_tiered.definition: The name 'paraffin' comes from Latin 'parum affinis' meaning 'little affinity', reflecting their low chemical reactivity.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Alkanes are unreactive because their C-C and C-H sigma bonds are strong and the molecules lack polarity, so they do not attract ionic or polar reagents.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Paraffin means 'not sticky' — alkanes don't grab onto acids or bases.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The strong, nonpolar single bonds in alkanes provide no site for electrophilic or nucleophilic attack under normal conditions.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Inertness arises from high bond dissociation energies (C-H ~413 kJ/mol, C-C ~348 kJ/mol) and the absence of a functional group. They resist oxidation (except combustion) and reduction.
reasoning_steps: Recall that alkanes consist only of single bonds.
Recognize that nonpolar molecules don't interact with polar reagents.
Conclude they have 'little affinity'.
misconceptions: Thinking alkanes react like alkenes (they do not).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Paraffins = unreactive due to strong C-C/C-H bonds.

glossary_entry.definition: Historical name for alkane meaning 'little affinity', indicating low reactivity.

flashcard.back: From Latin 'parum affinis' (little affinity), due to their low chemical reactivity.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Little affinity; alkanes are unreactive due to strong, nonpolar single bonds.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: An old term for alkane, meaning 'little affinity'.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Link to the Wurtz reaction difficulty.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Strong, nonpolar bonds cause low reactivity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.question: The balanced chemical equation for the complete combustion of propane (\(\mathrm{C_3H_8}\)) is:
questions.hint: Count the oxygen atoms: products need 10 O atoms (6 from CO2, 4 from H2O).
questions.explanation: Complete combustion requires 5 O2 molecules to balance: \(3\mathrm{CO_2}\) (6 O) + \(4\mathrm{H_2O}\) (4 O) = 10 O atoms = 5 O2. Option A underestimates oxygen needed.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES

explanations_tiered.definition: Complete combustion is the reaction of an alkane with excess oxygen to produce carbon dioxide and water.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For propane, 3 carbons yield 3 CO2, 8 hydrogens yield 4 H2O, requiring 10 oxygen atoms or 5 O2 molecules.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Propane needs 5 oxygen molecules to fully burn into 3 CO2 and 4 H2O.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using the general formula \(\mathrm{C_nH_{2n+2} + \frac{3n+1}{2}O_2}\), for n=3: \(\frac{10}{2}=5\) O2.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Balancing: \(\mathrm{C_3H_8 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 3CO_2 + 4H_2O}\). This is the standard enthalpy of combustion reaction.
reasoning_steps: Balance carbon: 3 CO2.
Balance hydrogen: 4 H2O.
Count oxygen on right: 6+4=10 O → 5 O2.
misconceptions: Forgetting to count oxygen from both CO2 and H2O simultaneously.

glossary_entry.definition: A chemical reaction between a fuel and an oxidant, producing heat and oxidation products.

flashcard.front: Balance combustion of propane: C3H8 + O2 → CO2 + H2O

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Balanced equation with 5 O2.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.question: During the photochemical chlorination of methane, free radicals are generated. What is the correct sequence of the mechanism?
questions.hint: The mechanism starts with breaking a bond and ends with radicals combining.
questions.explanation: Radical substitution proceeds: Initiation (\(\mathrm{Cl_2 \rightarrow 2Cl\cdot}\)), Propagation (reaction with methane), Termination (radical recombination). Option D incorrectly places termination before propagation.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.content_anchor: Chain Initiating Step, Chain Propagating Step, Chain Terminating Step

explanations_tiered.definition: Free radical substitution involves initiation (forming radicals), propagation (chain reaction), and termination (radical quenching).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Chlorination starts with Cl-Cl bond homolysis, then the Cl radical abstracts H from CH4, forming CH3Cl and continuing the chain.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Start with the spark (initiation), then the domino effect (propagation), then the stop (termination).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Step 1: \(\mathrm{Cl_2 \xrightarrow{hv} 2Cl\cdot}\). Step 2: \(\mathrm{Cl\cdot + CH_4 \rightarrow HCl + CH_3\cdot}\). Step 3: \(\mathrm{CH_3\cdot + Cl_2 \rightarrow CH_3Cl + Cl\cdot}\). Step 4: Radical combinations.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The quantum yield is high due to the chain propagation steps (2 and 3 repeating) before termination events (e.g., \(\mathrm{CH_3\cdot + Cl\cdot \rightarrow CH_3Cl}\)).
reasoning_steps: Identify the first step (bond breaking by light = initiation).
Identify the repeating steps (propagation).
Identify the final radical combination (termination).
misconceptions: Thinking termination happens after each propagation step.

glossary_entry.definition: An atom or molecule with an unpaired electron.

flashcard.front: What are the stages of free radical substitution?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Order of steps in free radical chlorination?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Initiation (bond break), Propagation (chain reaction), Termination (radical death).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A reaction pathway involving radicals, typically with initiation, propagation, and termination.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use arrows to show chain propagation.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Correct order is Initiation, Propagation, Termination.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.question: What is the main organic product when methane reacts with an excess of chlorine gas in the presence of ultraviolet light?

options.D: A mixture of chlorinated products including \(\mathrm{CCl_4}\)

questions.hint: Excess chlorine leads to multiple substitutions.
questions.explanation: Radical substitution continues with excess \(\mathrm{Cl_2}\), replacing all H atoms stepwise to yield \(\mathrm{CH_3Cl, CH_2Cl_2, CHCl_3,}\) and \(\mathrm{CCl_4}\). Option A is the product with limited chlorine.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES

explanations_tiered.definition: Halogenation of methane with excess chlorine leads to successive substitution forming a mixture of all possible chloromethanes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Once \(\mathrm{CH_3Cl}\) forms, it can undergo further chlorination to \(\mathrm{CH_2Cl_2}\), \(\mathrm{CHCl_3}\), and \(\mathrm{CCl_4}\) due to excess reagent.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Keep adding chlorine; it replaces one hydrogen, then another, until all four are replaced.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The mechanism does not stop at monochlorination if chlorine is abundant. All hydrogens are susceptible to radical abstraction, yielding a mixture.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The relative rates of hydrogen abstraction from \(\mathrm{CH_4}\) vs \(\mathrm{CH_3Cl}\) differ, but with large excess \(\mathrm{Cl_2}\), complete chlorination to \(\mathrm{CCl_4}\) is favored.
reasoning_steps: Recognize that radical substitution is not selective with excess reagent.
Determine that all C-H bonds can be replaced.
Identify the mixture result.
misconceptions: Thinking only one substitution occurs regardless of conditions.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Excess Cl2 + CH4 → mixture of CH3Cl, CH2Cl2, CHCl3, CCl4.

glossary_entry.definition: A substitution reaction where a hydrogen is replaced by a halogen.

flashcard.front: What happens when methane reacts with excess Cl2 in UV light?
flashcard.back: Mixture of CH3Cl, CH2Cl2, CHCl3, and CCl4.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Product of CH4 + excess Cl2 / UV?
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Excess chlorine yields a mixture.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.question: A student expects ethane to decolorize bromine water like ethene. Why does this expectation fail?

options.A: Ethane is a gas at room temperature
options.B: Ethane contains only single sigma bonds and does not undergo addition
options.C: Bromine only reacts with liquid hydrocarbons
options.D: Ethane is too polar to react with nonpolar bromine

questions.hint: Decolorization of bromine water tests for unsaturation (double/triple bonds).
questions.explanation: Alkanes like ethane are saturated; they lack the \(\pi\) bond needed for addition reactions with bromine. Option D is false because ethane is nonpolar, but reactivity is due to bond type, not polarity.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.content_anchor: Alkanes show a little chemical affinity

explanations_tiered.definition: Addition reactions require a \(\pi\) bond (double or triple bond), which alkanes lack.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Ethene decolorizes bromine via electrophilic addition across the double bond. Ethane has no \(\pi\) bond, so it cannot undergo addition.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Bromine 'opens' a double bond to attach; ethane has no 'opening'.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Alkanes undergo substitution (with light/heat), not addition. The test for unsaturation relies on the disappearance of bromine color via addition.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The electrophilic bromine molecule polarizes the \(\pi\) electron cloud of alkenes, leading to a cyclic bromonium ion intermediate. Alkanes lack this electron-rich site, requiring radical conditions for substitution.
reasoning_steps: Recall that bromine water tests for C=C double bonds.
Recognize ethane has no double bonds (only single bonds).
Conclude no reaction (no decolorization).
misconceptions: Assuming all hydrocarbons react the same way with bromine.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Only alkenes/alkynes decolorize Br2 (addition). Alkanes do not.

glossary_entry.definition: A chemical test for unsaturation where a brown solution becomes colorless if a double or triple bond is present.

flashcard.front: Why doesn't ethane decolorize bromine water?
flashcard.back: Ethane has no double/triple bond (saturated), so it cannot undergo addition.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Alkane vs Alkene with Br2 water?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Alkene decolorizes (addition); Alkane does not (needs light for substitution).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Using bromine water or KMnO4 to detect double/triple bonds.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common misconception: all hydrocarbons react with Br2.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. No double bond means no addition reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.question: How much heat is released per mole when 1.00 mol of methane (\(\mathrm{CH_4}\)) undergoes complete combustion to \(\mathrm{CO_2}\) and \(\mathrm{H_2O}\) if the standard enthalpy of combustion is \(-890 \text{ kJ/mol}\)?
questions.hint: The enthalpy of combustion is given as a negative value.
questions.explanation: A negative enthalpy of combustion (\(-890 \text{ kJ/mol}\)) indicates 890 kJ of heat is released per mole of methane combusted. Option C would be for 2 moles, and D states absorbed (endothermic), which is incorrect for combustion.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES

explanations_tiered.definition: Enthalpy of combustion is the heat released when one mole of a substance burns completely in oxygen.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: A negative \(\Delta H\) indicates an exothermic reaction. The magnitude (890) is the heat released per mole of methane.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The minus sign means heat goes 'out' (released).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(\mathrm{CH_4(g) + 2O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g) + 2H_2O(l)}\) releases 890 kJ of energy per mole.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Standard enthalpy of combustion (\(\Delta H_c^\circ\)) for methane is \(-890.8 \text{ kJ/mol}\), corresponding to the breaking and forming of bonds in the exothermic reaction.
worked_solution: Step 1: The problem gives \(\Delta H = -890 \text{ kJ/mol}\).
Step 2: Multiply by moles: \(1.00 \text{ mol} \times 890 \text{ kJ/mol} = 890 \text{ kJ}\).
Step 3: The negative sign implies heat is released.
reasoning_steps: Interpret the negative sign (exothermic = released).
Multiply by the number of moles (1).
Select the value with 'released'.

glossary_entry.definition: The heat energy released when one mole of a substance undergoes complete combustion.

flashcard.front: Heat released by 1 mol CH4 (ΔH = -890 kJ/mol)?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: ΔH_comb CH4 = -890 kJ/mol. Heat released for 1 mol?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A reaction that releases heat, indicated by a negative ΔH.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Write combustion equations and interpret enthalpy
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Negative ΔH implies 890 kJ released.
